title: Published /.well-known/security.txt has an expired Expires date (2025-12-31)
author: kobihikri
body:
### Describe the Issue

The published `security.txt` at `https://www.freecodecamp.org/.well-known/security.txt` (and the apex `freecodecamp.org`) has an `Expires` date that has passed:

```
Contact: https://contribute.freecodecamp.org/security
Expires: 2025-12-31T23:59:00Z
```

Today is 2026-07-14, so the file expired ~6.5 months ago. Per [RFC 9116 §2.5.5](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9116#section-2.5.5), a researcher (or automated tooling) is expected to treat a `security.txt` past its `Expires` as stale — which quietly undermines your own vulnerability-reporting surface, even though the `Contact` link still works.

### What I found about the fix location (so you don't have to dig)

I looked for the source file to point at a fix, but it isn't in a public repo anymore:
- Not in `freeCodeCamp/freeCodeCamp` (removed in #57962 / #57966, "moved downstream, served from NGINX").
- In `freeCodeCamp/nginx-config` it was removed in #116; the remaining placeholder says the file is managed "at the edge" (Cloudflare).

So this looks like a Cloudflare edge-config update on your side (bump `Expires` to a future date, ideally with a renewal reminder), not a code PR I can send. I'm raising it as a heads-up so the advertised file stays valid.

### Reproduction

```
curl -sSL https://www.freecodecamp.org/.well-known/security.txt   # Expires: 2025-12-31T23:59:00Z (past)
```

I used AI assistance to help investigate and draft this; I reproduced the facts myself. Thank you for all the work on freeCodeCamp.

title: Text out of synch with speech
author: Supravisor
body:
### Describe the Issue

In the video, the text transitions to the next part of the speech before the audio for the current text has finished.

### Affected Page

https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/b1-english-for-developers/learn-how-to-talk-about-numbers-with-a-coworker/dialogue-3-inventory-check

### Your code

```



```


### Expected behavior

The current text should stay on the page while the associated audio is running.

### Screenshots

_No response_

### System

- Device: [e.g. iPhone 6, Laptop]
- OS: [e.g. iOS 14, Windows 10, Ubuntu 20.04]
- Browser: [e.g. Chrome, Safari]
- Version: [e.g. 22]


### Additional context

_No response_

title: Lack of white space causing tests to fail for `else` statement
author: Supravisor
body: ### Describe the Issue The `else` statement fails when white space is not present before and/or after the opening and closing curly brace. ### Affected Page https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/javascript-v9/workshop-logic-checker-app/step-12 ### Your code ``` }else{ console.log("Timmy is not old enough to drive.") } ``` ``` } else{ console.log("Timmy is not old enough to drive.") } ``` ``` }else { console.log("Timmy is not old enough to drive.") } ``` ### Expected behavior Test should pass when the code is correct. ### Screenshots ### System - Device: [e.g. iPhone 6, Laptop] - OS: [e.g. iOS 14, Windows 10, Ubuntu 20.04] - Browser: [e.g. Chrome, Safari] - Version: [e.g. 22] ### Additional context _No response_

title: Format examples as bulleted list
author: huyenltnguyen
body:
## Description

Format the examples as a bulleted list and also improve the wording a bit.

## Changes needed

Change:

https://github.com/freeCodeCamp/freeCodeCamp/blob/005a967c95dadc5924a6a683be4aff07cab8b00b/curriculum/challenges/english/blocks/learn-how-to-clarify-information-in-different-interactions/6633e43c32afebfaef45da26.md#L13

To:

```
When talking about the clothes a person has on, you normally use a combination of the verb `to be` with the verb `wear` in the `-ing` form. For example: 

- `Paul is wearing jeans and a T-shirt.` 
- `I am wearing a blue jacket.`
```

title: Text issues in the Build a Travel Agency Page lab (missing article, charset casing, indentation)
author: majestic-owl448
body:
### Describe the Issue

There are three small text issues in the "Build a Travel Agency Page" lab.

1. One hint is missing the indefinite article before `title`:

> You should have `title` element within your `head` element.

2. The `charset` value is written two different ways in the same challenge. User story 3 in the description uses `utf-8`:

> You should have a `head` element containing a `meta` void element with `charset` set to `utf-8` and a `title` with the text `Travel Agency Page`.

but the hint for that requirement uses `UTF-8`:

> One `meta` element should have its `charset` attribute set to `UTF-8`.

The solution code uses `utf-8`, so the hint is the odd one out.

3. The code fence for one hint (and the assertion inside it) is indented by a single space, while every other hint block in the file sits flush left. This is the block that starts on line 60 and ends on line 64.

### Affected Pages

https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/responsive-web-design-v9/lab-travel-agency-page/build-a-travel-agency-page

### Expected behavior

1. Add the article `a` so the hint reads: ``You should have a `title` element within your `head` element.``

2. Change `UTF-8` to `utf-8` in the hint on line 66, so it matches the description and the solution code.

3. Remove the single leading space from the opening code fence and its contents (lines 60 through 64) so the block is flush left like the other hint blocks.

title: Fix grammar and code-tag issues in the Basic HTML catalog intro copy
author: majestic-owl448
body: ### Describe the Issue 1. In the catalog intro copy for the "Build a Travel Agency Page" lab, the word `anchor` is wrapped in `` tags: ``` In this lab, you'll review working with HTML fundamentals by creating a web page for a travel agency. You'll work with images, the figure element, the figcaption element, the anchor element, and more. ``` `figure` and `figcaption` are real HTML tag names, so wrapping them in `` is correct. `anchor` is not a tag name. The element is ``, informally called the anchor element. Presenting `anchor` as code implies it is a valid tag, which it is not. 2. In the catalog intro copy for the "Working with Audio and Video Elements" lecture, the text uses the plural "lectures": ``` In these lectures, you will learn how to work with the audio and video elements. ``` The block contains a single lecture challenge, so "these lectures" is a number mismatch. 3. In the catalog intro copy for the "Working with Images and SVGs" lecture, the word `learn` appears twice in the same sentence: ``` In these lectures, you will learn how to work with SVGs and learn about techniques for optimizing your images. ``` The repeated `learn` is redundant. 4. In the catalog intro copy for the "Build a Heart Icon" workshop, the final sentence is missing its terminal period: ``` In this workshop, you will practice working with SVGs by building a heart icon ``` Every other block intro in this catalog ends its final sentence with a period. 5. In the catalog intro copy for the "Working with Links" lecture, a comma splits a single list item: ``` In these lectures, you will learn about links, the target attribute, different link states, absolute, and relative paths, and more. ``` `absolute, and relative paths` puts a comma inside "absolute and relative paths", so it reads as two separate items rather than one. ### Affected Pages https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/basic-html/#lab-travel-agency-page https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/basic-html/#lecture-working-with-audio-and-video-elements https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/basic-html/#lecture-working-with-images-and-svgs https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/basic-html/#workshop-build-a-heart-icon https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/basic-html/#lecture-working-with-links ### Expected behavior 1. Change `anchor` to `a` so the code tag holds the real tag name, matching how `figure` and `figcaption` are used in the same sentence. 2. Change `In these lectures` to `In this lecture`. 3. Change `and learn about techniques for optimizing` to `and explore techniques for optimizing`. 4. Add a period to the end of the sentence so it reads `by building a heart icon.` 5. Change `absolute, and relative paths` to `absolute and relative paths` (remove the comma after `absolute`).

title: Fix curly apostrophe and missing serial comma in the Basic HTML Quiz
author: majestic-owl448
body:
### Describe the Issue

1. A distractor uses a curly apostrophe:

   > It specifies the audio file’s source URL.

2. A question uses a three-item list without the serial comma:

   > Which of the following elements is used to convey a sense of urgency, seriousness or strong importance?

### Affected Pages

https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/responsive-web-design-v9/quiz-basic-html/quiz-basic-html

### Expected behavior

1. Change `file’s` to `file's` so it uses a straight apostrophe.
2. Change `seriousness or strong importance` to `seriousness, or strong importance` so the three-item list uses the serial comma.

title: Fix missing elements, heading case, punctuation, and terminology gaps in the Basic HTML Review
author: majestic-owl448
body:
### Describe the Issue

1. The review never mentions the `button` element. It is taught in the ["Basic HTML" bookstore page workshop](https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/responsive-web-design-v9/workshop-bookstore-page/step-13) as an interactive element for creating clickable buttons, but the "Common HTML elements" section lists the other common elements and leaves it out.

2. The review never mentions the `autoplay` attribute. The ["What Are the Roles of the HTML Audio and Video Elements, and How Do They Work?"](https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/responsive-web-design-v9/lecture-working-with-audio-and-video-elements/what-are-the-roles-of-the-html-audio-and-video-elements) lecture teaches it as a `video` attribute that plays the video automatically, but the multimedia section lists `controls`, `loop`, `muted`, `poster`, and `source` without it.

3. Three section headings use sentence case while the other eight use title case:

   > ## Common HTML elements

   > ## Target attribute types

   > ## Link states

4. A two-item list uses an incorrect comma:

   > There are some other replaced elements, such as `video`, and `embed`.

   A list of two items takes no comma before the conjunction.

5. The definition of an absolute path folds in the protocol and domain name:

   > An absolute path also includes the protocol, which could be `http`, `https`, and `file` and the domain name if the resource is on the web.

   The ["What Is the Difference Between Absolute and Relative Paths?"](https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/responsive-web-design-v9/lecture-working-with-links/what-is-the-difference-between-absolute-and-relative-paths) lecture separates an absolute path (a location within a file system) from an absolute URL (which adds the protocol and domain name). The review merges the two and never introduces the term absolute URL.

6. The list of `target` attribute values is missing the serial comma:

   > There are four important possible values for this attribute: `_self`, `_blank`, `_parent` and `_top`.

7. The ID rules do not mention that IDs may contain underscores:

   > ID names cannot have spaces. If your ID name contains multiple words then you can use dashes between the words like this:

   The ["What Are IDs and Classes, and When Should You Use Them?"](https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/responsive-web-design-v9/lecture-html-fundamentals/what-are-ids-and-classes) lecture states that id values may contain letters, digits, underscores, and dashes. The review mentions only spaces and dashes, which leaves a camper unable to answer a quiz question that depends on underscores being allowed.

### Affected Pages

https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/responsive-web-design-v9/review-basic-html/basic-html-review

### Expected behavior

1. The `button` element is summarized in the "Common HTML elements" section.
2. The `autoplay` attribute is summarized alongside the other audio and video attributes.
3. The three headings match the title case used by the rest of the section headings.
4. The two-item list reads without the extra comma.
5. The review distinguishes an absolute path from an absolute URL, matching the lecture it summarizes.
6. The list of `target` values uses the serial comma.
7. The ID rules note that underscores are allowed.

title: Fix redundant wording and quiz answers missing the focus state in Working with Links lectures
author: majestic-owl448
body:
### Describe the Issue

1. In the "What Is the Difference Between Slashes, a Single Dot, and Double Dot in Path Syntax?" lecture, a sentence repeats the same idea twice:

> Remember that you learned in a previous lesson about relative versus absolute paths before.

`in a previous lesson` and `before` say the same thing.

2. In the same lecture, a sentence is awkwardly phrased:

> Double dots, however, are much more common to access files outside of the current working directory.

`more common to access` is not idiomatic.

3. In the "What Are the Different Link States, and Why Are They Important?" lecture, the correct answer to the ordering question drops the `focus` state:

> `link`, `visited`, `hover`, `active`.

The lecture body teaches a five-state order: `link`, `visited`, `hover`, `focus`, then `active`. The correct answer and all three distractors list only four states and omit `focus`, so no option matches the order the lecture teaches.

### Affected Pages

https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/responsive-web-design-v9/lecture-working-with-links/what-is-the-difference-between-slashes-a-single-dot-and-double-dot-in-path-syntax

https://www.freecodecamp.org/learn/responsive-web-design-v9/lecture-working-with-links/what-are-the-different-link-states

### Expected behavior

1. Change `Remember that you learned in a previous lesson about relative versus absolute paths before.` to `Remember that you learned about relative versus absolute paths in a previous lesson.`

2. Change `much more common to access files` to `more commonly used to access files`.

3. Add `focus` to every option so each lists the same five states in a different order, and set the correct answer to ``` `link`, `visited`, `hover`, `focus`, `active`. ```
